content.text: Git is a distributed version control system that tracks every change made to a codebase, lets you revert mistakes, work in parallel branches, and collaborate with teammates without overwriting each other's work. For React projects it is the single most important tool outside the language itself.

attrs.alt: Git data flow diagram: Working Directory → Staging Area → Local Repo → Remote Repo, with feature branch workflow and .gitignore examples
attrs.title: Git data flow and feature branch workflow

content.text: A repository (repo) is a directory tracked by Git. The
folder at the root stores the entire history, configuration, and object database. You never edit
directly — all interaction goes through git commands.
Git separates your work into three distinct areas — understanding this model resolves most beginner confusion:
Files chosen for the next commit — a draft snapshot
A commit is a permanent, content-addressed snapshot of the entire staging area at a point in time. Git identifies each commit by a SHA-1 hash (e.g.
). Every commit records its parent commit(s), forming a linked history. Commits are immutable — you never change one in place.
A branch is a lightweight movable pointer to a commit. Creating a branch is instant — it just writes a 41-byte file. The default branch is typically named
is a special pointer that tracks which branch (or commit) you are currently on.
Configure your identity once per machine — Git attaches this to every commit you make:

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
Starting a React Project with Git

npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts
cd my-app
git init
git add .
git commit -m "initial commit"
and the build output folder. Check it before the first commit to make sure nothing sensitive is tracked.

git clone https://github.com/org/repo.git
cd repo
npm install      # restore node_modules — not tracked in git

git status            # show modified, staged, and untracked files
git log --oneline     # compact commit history
git log --oneline --graph --all   # visualise all branches
git diff              # unstaged changes vs last commit
git diff --staged     # staged changes vs last commit

git add src/App.tsx               # stage one file
git add src/                      # stage entire folder
git add -p                        # stage interactively, hunk by hunk
git commit -m "feat: add login form"
git commit --amend                # rewrite the last commit (before push only)
Many React teams follow Conventional Commits:
. This format is machine-readable and powers automated changelogs.

git restore src/App.tsx           # discard working-dir changes (permanent)
git restore --staged src/App.tsx  # unstage without losing changes
git revert <sha>                  # create a new commit that undoes <sha>
git reset --soft HEAD~1           # undo last commit, keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1           # undo last commit, DISCARD changes (dangerous)

git branch                        # list local branches
git branch feature/dark-mode      # create branch
git switch feature/dark-mode      # switch to it
git switch -c feature/dark-mode   # create + switch in one command
git branch -d feature/dark-mode   # delete after merge (-D to force)
still works but does double duty (switching branches AND restoring files), which makes intent ambiguous.

git switch main
git merge feature/dark-mode       # fast-forward if no divergence
git merge --no-ff feature/dark-mode  # always create a merge commit
A fast-forward merge simply moves the branch pointer forward.
forces a merge commit even when fast-forward is possible — useful for preserving the existence of feature branches in history.

git switch feature/dark-mode
git rebase main               # replay feature commits on top of main

Rebase rewrites history — the feature commits get new SHA hashes. It produces a cleaner linear history but should only be used on branches not yet shared with others. Never rebase a branch that teammates have pulled.
When two branches edit the same lines, Git marks conflicts in the file:
Edit the file to keep what you want (remove the conflict markers), then:

git add src/App.tsx
git commit            # or git rebase --continue if rebasing
VS Code highlights conflict markers with Accept Current / Accept Incoming / Accept Both buttons. Most modern editors do the same.

git remote -v                          # show configured remotes
git remote add origin <url>            # link to GitHub/GitLab repo
git push -u origin main               # push and set tracking branch
git push                              # subsequent pushes (shorthand)
git pull                              # fetch + merge remote changes
git fetch origin                      # fetch without merging
flag on the first push sets the upstream so future
know which remote branch to target.

git switch -c feature/shopping-cart
# ... make commits ...
git push -u origin feature/shopping-cart
# open a pull request on GitHub
# after merge, delete the remote branch and clean up locally:
git switch main
git pull
git branch -d feature/shopping-cart

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# Build output
dist/
build/
.vite/

# Environment files (contain secrets)
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local

# Editor files
.DS_Store
*.log

# Test coverage
coverage/
before adding the ignore rule, remove it from tracking without deleting it locally:

git rm -r --cached node_modules/
git commit -m "chore: stop tracking node_modules"
You're mid-feature and need to switch branches to fix an urgent bug. Use
to shelve the changes without committing:

git stash                         # shelve current changes
git switch main
# fix the bug, commit
git switch feature/dark-mode
git stash pop                     # restore shelved changes

git stash list                    # see all stashes
git stash push -m "wip: modal"   # named stash
git stash drop stash@{0}          # remove a specific stash

git tag v1.0.0                    # lightweight tag
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release"   # annotated tag (includes message + tagger)
git push origin v1.0.0            # push the tag
git push origin --tags            # push all tags
Annotated tags are recommended for releases — they store the tagger's name, date, and a message, and they appear correctly in
output used by many CI tools.

git log --oneline -10              # last 10 commits
git log --author="Alice"           # commits by author
git log -- src/App.tsx             # commits that touched a file
git show a3f2c1d                   # show diff for a specific commit
git blame src/App.tsx              # line-by-line last editor
git bisect start                   # binary search for the commit that introduced a bug
When a bug exists now but didn't before,
performs a binary search through the commit history to find the exact commit that introduced it:

git bisect start
git bisect bad                 # current commit has the bug
git bisect good v1.0.0         # this tag was clean
# Git checks out a midpoint commit. Test your app, then:
git bisect good                # or git bisect bad
# Repeat until Git prints: "<sha> is the first bad commit"
git bisect reset               # return to original HEAD
Adds hundreds of MB to the repo and causes conflicts on every
API keys and secrets pushed to GitHub become permanently public even if deleted later. Use
rewrites shared history and can erase teammates' commits. Only force-push personal feature branches that nobody else has pulled.
Giant all-at-once commits are hard to review and bisect. Commit logical units — a component addition, a bug fix, a style change — separately.
and you rebase it, their local copy now has a diverged history. Coordinate before rebasing shared branches.
) before pushing to avoid rejected pushes.

[alias]
  st   = status
  co   = switch
  lg   = log --oneline --graph --all
  unstage = restore --staged
  oops = commit --amend --no-edit
GitHub and GitLab add a layer on top of Git: the pull request (PR) or merge request (MR). This is the standard review gate before code enters
Push your feature branch to origin.
Open a pull request on GitHub: base =
Teammates review the diff and leave comments. Address each by pushing new commits to the same branch — the PR updates automatically.
CI runs (tests, lint, type-check). All checks must pass.
Merge (squash, merge commit, or rebase) and delete the remote branch.
Merge commit + all branch commits visible
One commit on main, branch commits hidden
Clean main history, messy branch WIP commits
Branch commits replayed onto main, linear
Clean history with individual commit context
Cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the entire branch:

# Apply commit a3f2c1d from any branch onto the current branch:
git cherry-pick a3f2c1d

# Cherry-pick a range (exclusive of start, inclusive of end):
git cherry-pick abc123..def456
Common use case: a hotfix commit made on a release branch also needs to appear on
without merging the entire release branch.
Before opening a PR, you can clean up a messy commit history with interactive rebase:
git rebase -i HEAD~4    # edit the last 4 commits
Your editor opens with a list of commits. Available actions per line:
— keep the commit but edit its message.
— fold this commit into the previous one.
Interactive rebase rewrites history. Only run it on commits that have not been pushed, or on a personal feature branch no one else has pulled.
GitHub supports two protocols for push/pull. SSH is more convenient for daily use:
Easy start, prompts for token on first push
One-time key generation + upload to GitHub; no passwords after

# Generate SSH key (one-time)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@example.com"
# Copy public key
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
# Add to GitHub: Settings → SSH keys → New SSH key
# Test connection
ssh -T git@github.com
Switch an existing HTTPS clone to SSH:
Git hooks are shell scripts that run automatically at specific points in the Git workflow. They live in
but are not tracked by Git. Teams share hooks via
(tracked) and points Git to that folder.
Run lint-staged, format staged files with Prettier
Validate Conventional Commit format with commitlint

# Install Husky
npm install --save-dev husky
npx husky init

# .husky/pre-commit — lint staged files:
npx lint-staged

git pull                   # fetch + merge
# or, to keep a linear history:
git pull --rebase
"Updates were rejected because the remote contains work"
Someone else pushed to the same branch. Pull first, resolve any conflicts, then push:

git pull --rebase origin main
git push
Accidentally committed to main instead of a feature branch

# Move the commit to a new branch, reset main
git switch -c feature/oops
git switch main
git reset --hard HEAD~1   # remove the accidental commit from main
This happens when you check out a commit SHA directly instead of a branch name. Commits made here are orphaned when you switch away. To save work:
git switch -c rescue-branch    # create a branch pointing to current detached commit
Git tracks changes through three areas (working directory, staging, repository) and communicates with remote repos via push and pull. The core daily loop is:
. Feature branches isolate work until it is ready to merge, and a well-maintained
keeps build artifacts and secrets out of the repository.
